analyst_observations: The skill achieves 100% pass rate across all assertions vs 85.5% baseline — the 3 failures without the skill were: missing screen thickness analysis (audit), missing helpers pattern (scaffold), and missing 'thin screen' framing (code review).

The baseline performs surprisingly well (85.5%) because the project's CLAUDE.md already contains detailed architecture rules. The skill's advantage is in the screaming-architecture-specific concepts: screen thickness tracking, Convex helpers pattern, and the 'thin screen' mental model.

The skill runs ~41% slower and uses ~10% more tokens. This is expected — agents with the skill read reference files (expo-patterns.md, convex-patterns.md, audit-report-template.md) before doing work. The tradeoff is worthwhile given the quality improvement.

Non-discriminating assertions: 'detects-expo-project-type', 'detects-convex-backend', 'identifies-feature-domains' pass for both configurations — these test basic project understanding, not skill-specific value.

The with-skill audit used the structured report template (score, violation/warning/note categories, detailed section tables) while the without-skill audit used a more freeform structure. Both were readable but the templated version is more consistent and scannable.

For the scaffold eval, the key differentiator was the Convex helpers pattern — the skill explicitly teaches separating business logic into helpers.ts with QueryCtx/MutationCtx, while the baseline put all logic inline in handlers. This mirrors the actual screaming architecture principle of separating concerns within the backend too.
